title: The Macro Minute: Is the consensus bullishness regarding the earnings outlook justified?

Happy Thursday out there team, 42 with
your skipper here at Darius Dale to
present our macro minute for Thursday,
April 23rd, 2026. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
lead off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
is the consensus bullishness regarding
the earnings outlook justified? Our
answer is absolutely.
Among S&P 500 constituents, reported
revenue growth is accelerating to a near
4-year high, and reported earnings
growth is stable and comfortably above
its long-run mean.
Moreover, sales and EPS rates are
accelerating and and comfortably above
their long-run means as well. Estimates
for sales and earnings growth in 2026
are currently at cycle highs, and the
modest pullback in estimates for sales
and earnings growth in 2027 leaves both
figures at or above their respective
3-month moving averages. In totality,
this is as good as corporate profit
cycle dynamics have been since early
2021,
which strongly supports our bullish
paradigm C, aka run it hot, uh
productivity boom, and jobless recovery
themes.
Smart money bears better hope the Strait
of Hormuz remains offline for the
foreseeable future, because us dumb
money bulls will leave them in the dust
for the fourth year in a row, and six of
the past eight when factoring in 2019
and 2020.
As always, I'll wrap up with the
question from our community. This one's
titled L and M in today, says in today's
L and M in you lead off morning note,
you mentioned that the consensus expects
a year-end close of about 7,400 for the
S&P 500. Does 42 macro agree with this
consensus or
or is estimates or are estimates too low
based on consensus factoring in too low
of a growth number compared to 42
macro's estimate? So, a great question.
So, the first thing I'll say is we're
about 50% higher than consensus on GDP
growth for 2026 and 2027. So, in our
opinion, we think those GDP growth
numbers are going to get revised higher
from consensus standpoint over the next
over the medium term, and in our
opinion, we think that is supportive of
a continued positive revision cycle for
earnings and sales estimates. And so,
that's that's all positive stuff, but as
it relates to this target, I have no
idea what the market's going to close at
year-end [laughter]
2026 or I don't know what the market's
going to close today. How in the world
is anybody going to
December 31st forecast? I mean, I'm
telling you right now, there's not a
credible buy-sider in the world that
forecast that that invests based on
year-end price targets for the S&P 500.
Not one, not a single one, I guarantee
you. In fact, the only one I've ever
seen in my career, almost two decades on
global Wall Street, was the late great
Leon Cooperman over at Omega Advisors.
Our first meeting with him, this is
probably this is probably 15 years ago,
our first meeting with him, the first
question he asked me was,
"What's your year-end price target?" And
I literally was like,
you know, we don't have one. I don't we
don't have a year-end price target.
So, you can imagine how the rest of the
meeting went, but the key takeaway is
that,
you know, leave the pin the tail on the
donkey stuff like this for the sell
side. If your goal is to try to stay on
the right side of market risk, which is
what we do here at 42 macro, you don't
need any of this stuff. Just understand
what the rates of change are for the six
key macro cycles, and if you're a
bottom-up investor, you need to
understand the rates of change relative
to consensus for sales, margins, and
earnings. So, we'll wrap it up there.
Darius Dale here presenting our macro
minute for Thursday, April 23rd, 2026.
Best of luck out there today. We'll
catch you back here tomorrow. Cheers.
